our_argument_summary: Our review argues that hemispheric asymmetry in geomagnetic field decay is explained by reversed-flux patches in the outer core, characterized by CHAOS-7 and derived from first principles via MHD geodynamo simulations (Aubert et al., 2013). The dome model specifies no numerical ratio, no time period, and no derivation from dome geometry — making the claim unfalsifiable. The dome's H(r) function is axially symmetric and cannot produce hemispheric asymmetry without breaking its own symmetry. Verdict: Std Model Explains.

cycle_2_notes.change_magnitude: major — detail_evidence expanded from 486 to 2279 chars (4.7× increase), verdict text from 266 to 373 chars. Most Cycle 1 recommendations were applied.
c1_fixes_applied: CHAOS-7 vs first-principles distinction now correctly made — detail_evidence separates CHAOS-7 (data product) from MHD geodynamo simulations (explanatory theory)
Vagueness argument added — text now explicitly calls out missing numerical ratio, time period, hemispheric definition, and physical mechanism
Axial symmetry argument added — H(r) radial dependence noted as mathematically incompatible with hemispheric asymmetry
Finlay et al. (2020) DOI added as formal citation alongside CHAOS-7 repository
Aubert et al. (2013) Nature citation added for geodynamo first-principles basis
Toroidal aetheric flow counterargument addressed — acknowledged dome has directional structure but no published formula connecting it to a specific ratio
NOAA WMM data reuse from WIN-004/005 explicitly noted
c1_fixes_not_applied: The WIN-005 re-slicing argument was NOT added. The detail text does not note that WIN-063 is arithmetic on WIN-005's already-counted data. This was flagged as a 'stronger argument' in Cycle 1 but the current text only mentions the group membership, not the specific redundancy with WIN-005.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt — WIN-005 mentions 'Asymmetric decay predicted by aetheric field geometry' with CHAOS-7 source. WIN-063 itself does not appear in the extracted V50.6 raw text (it's a V51.0 addition).
No monitor.py file in raw-text directory — prior group member reviews (WIN-040 through WIN-061) confirmed no monitoring domain for asymmetry ratio metrics.
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline exists. WIN-063 is a V51.0 addition that is not present in the V50.6 raw text extraction. Its claim appears to repackage the WIN-005 asymmetric decay observation as a 'ratio' metric. No formula for deriving the asymmetry ratio from dome parameters (H(r), disc_radius, aetheric vortex topology) exists on the dome site.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring domain computes or validates an asymmetry ratio. The WIN doesn't appear in monitor.py based on group member reviews.
data_sources_used: NOAA WMM (generic — same data as WIN-004/005/035)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome specifies no numerical ratio, no time period, and no definition of 'hemispheric' on flat disc geometry. Without committing to a predicted value, any observed asymmetry automatically 'matches.' The word 'matches' implies quantitative agreement but no quantitative prediction is stated.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review makes no code-specific claims about WIN-063 monitoring. The absence of a monitoring domain is correctly implied by the 'none' code_analysis tag.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-063 is a V51.0 addition not present in the V50.6 raw text extraction. No monitoring domain was identified in prior reviews of all group members (WIN-040 through WIN-061). The dome's monitor.py has geomagnetic domains that check SAA field strength thresholds and pole position, but none computes a hemispheric decay asymmetry ratio. WIN-005 (which shares the asymmetry theme) uses a hardcoded field strength threshold, not a ratio computation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Hemispheric asymmetry in geomagnetic field decay is established in geomagnetism since at least Gubbins (1987, Nature). CHAOS-7 (Finlay et al., 2020) characterizes it via time-dependent spherical harmonic expansions. Reversed-flux patches beneath South Africa drive differential secular variation. The dome model renames the mechanism as 'aetheric field geometry' without providing an alternative derivation or different numerical prediction.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Hemispheric asymmetry in field decay documented since Gubbins (1987). CHAOS-7 and predecessors have quantified it since 2006. WIN-063 is new in V51.0 (2026), adopting a phenomenon documented for ~40 years. WIN-005 (predating WIN-063) already cited the same African/American cell decay asymmetry from CHAOS-7 data.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km is purely radial (axially symmetric). An axially symmetric geometry cannot derive hemispheric (angular) asymmetry — this is a mathematical constraint, not an empirical argument. The dome's toroidal aetheric flow has potential directional structure, but no published formula connects it to a specific asymmetry ratio. No derivation path from dome geometry → numerical asymmetry ratio exists.

holes_found.description: The Aubert et al. (2013) citation (doi:10.1038/nature12574) may not be the best reference for hemispheric asymmetry specifically. That paper ('Bottom-up control of geomagnetic secular variation by the Earth's inner core') focuses on inner core control of secular variation, not specifically on hemispheric asymmetry of decay. A more targeted citation would be Olson & Deguen (2012, Nature Geoscience) on lopsided inner core growth causing dipole eccentricity, or Terra-Nova et al. (2017, PEPI) on the South Atlantic Anomaly and reversed-flux patches. The current text says Aubert et al. 'reproduce hemispheric asymmetry via core-mantle boundary heat flux variations and reversed-flux patches' — this may be a stretch of what that specific paper demonstrates. A dome defender with access to the paper could challenge whether it actually shows what we claim.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the Aubert et al. (2013) paper content. If it does not specifically discuss hemispheric asymmetry in decay rates, either (a) replace with a more targeted reference (e.g., Olson & Deguen 2012, Terra-Nova et al. 2017), or (b) add the qualifier '(and related geodynamo studies)' to avoid overstating what one paper shows. The parenthetical caveat already added ('Note: these simulations are data-constrained numerical models...') is good but doesn't fix the attribution if the paper doesn't discuss hemispheric asymmetry.

holes_found.description: The WIN-005 re-slicing argument is missing from the current text. WIN-005 already claims 'African SAA cell decays faster' using CHAOS-7 asymmetric decay data. WIN-063 takes the same observation and repackages it as a 'ratio.' This was identified as a stronger argument in Cycle 1 but was not incorporated. The detail_group field shows 'WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063' and the text mentions 'NOAA WMM data already cited in WIN-004/005,' but it doesn't explicitly say WIN-063 is redundant with WIN-005. For the 'Misleading' verdict consideration: WIN-041 and WIN-042 were both tagged 'Misleading' for similar re-slicing of existing data. WIN-063 does the same thing (takes WIN-005's asymmetry observation and presents it as a new 'ratio' metric) — there's a case this should be 'Misleading' rather than 'Std Model Explains.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence to detail_evidence: 'WIN-063 extracts a ratio from the same CHAOS-7 asymmetric decay data already cited in WIN-005 (African cell decays faster than American cell). Computing a ratio from one WIN's data does not constitute an independent prediction.' Alternatively, consider whether verdict should be 'Misleading' (re-slicing) rather than 'Std Model Explains,' since the dominant issue here is that it's a recycled observation, not just that standard physics explains it. However, the current verdict is defensible — the argument could go either way.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence text includes a long parenthetical aside about data-constrained numerical models: '(Note: these simulations are data-constrained numerical models, not purely first-principles derivations — but they are derived from Maxwell's equations and Navier-Stokes, with boundary conditions from seismology.)' This is accurate and intellectually honest, but it's a defensive caveat that hands a dome defender ammunition: 'See, even they admit their models need boundary conditions from data — just like ours.' The caveat is correct but could be framed more carefully.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe the parenthetical to emphasize the key distinction: data-constrained geodynamo models derive the asymmetry from physical laws (Maxwell + Navier-Stokes) with independently measured boundary conditions — the dome model doesn't derive anything at all. Suggested: '(Note: geodynamo simulations use independently measured boundary conditions from seismology, but the asymmetry emerges from the physics of fluid convection, not from fitting the asymmetry itself.)'

holes_found.description: The text says 'The dome needs to commit to a predicted ratio (e.g., NH decays 1.4× faster than SH over the next 10 years) before observation, not after.' While the point is valid, the specific example value '1.4×' was invented by us and could be misread as a value the dome claims. A dome defender could quote this out of context: 'The reviewers themselves estimate NH decays 1.4× faster, consistent with our model.' The example is illustrative but creates a minor attack surface.
holes_found.recommendation: Change to a more clearly hypothetical framing: '(e.g., "the NH dipole moment will decrease X% faster than the SH over the period 2025-2035")' — using a variable rather than a specific number avoids the risk of the example being mistaken for or quoted as an endorsement of a specific value.

stronger_arguments.description: The verdict could potentially be 'Misleading' rather than 'Std Model Explains.' WIN-041 (SAA multi-station decay) and WIN-042 (field decay ≥28 nT/year) both received 'Misleading' verdicts for being re-sliced versions of existing observations. WIN-063 follows the same pattern: it takes WIN-005's asymmetric decay observation and repackages it as a 'ratio.' The primary defect is not that standard physics explains it (though it does), but that it's a recycled data point masquerading as an independent confirmation. On the other hand, 'Std Model Explains' is also defensible since the core physics explanation is the strongest rebuttal. The current verdict is fine but the re-slicing angle should be more prominent in the evidence text.
sources: WIN-005 detail in wins.json (same CHAOS-7 asymmetric decay data)
WIN-041 and WIN-042 verdicts (Misleading for similar re-slicing)

stronger_arguments.description: The axial symmetry argument could be made even more forceful by noting the logical trap: if the dome breaks its axial symmetry to explain hemispheric asymmetry, it introduces new free parameters — which directly contradicts the 'zero fitted parameters' claim made in Section 6.1 (Part 6, post-V6 numbering). The dome can't have it both ways: either H(r) is axially symmetric (and can't produce hemispheric asymmetry) or the model has angular-dependent parameters (and the 'zero fitted parameters' claim is false). This is already in the Cycle 1 deeper_analysis but hasn't been added to the published text.

kernel_of_truth.description: Hemispheric asymmetry in geomagnetic field decay is a real, scientifically important observation. The South Atlantic Anomaly and reversed-flux patches beneath southern Africa produce measurable differences between hemispheres that are still being actively researched.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Observing a real phenomenon does not constitute a prediction. The dome model provides no formula, no derivation from dome geometry, and no specific numerical ratio derived before measurement. Any model of the geomagnetic field must account for hemispheric asymmetry — doing so is necessary but insufficient. Standard geomagnetism explains the mechanism (core-mantle boundary heat flux heterogeneity driving reversed-flux patches) with testable, falsifiable predictions. The dome's 'aetheric field geometry' explanation provides no comparable testable mechanism — and its own H(r) function is axially symmetric, mathematically precluding hemispheric asymmetry derivation.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 assessment: The text was substantially expanded since Cycle 1, incorporating 6 of 7 major Cycle 1 recommendations. The review is now much stronger than V1. The remaining gap is the WIN-005 re-slicing argument — this is the most important missing piece because it addresses a different kind of defect (recycled data point) than the currently emphasized mechanism-level argument (standard physics explains it).

The Aubert et al. (2013) citation concern is new for Cycle 2. The DOI resolves and the paper is real, but its specific content may not directly address hemispheric decay asymmetry in the way our text implies. This needs verification — if the paper is about inner core control rather than hemispheric asymmetry, our attribution could be challenged. The fix is easy (add or substitute a more targeted reference) but requires confirming what the paper actually says.

The verdict question (Std Model Explains vs. Misleading) is a legitimate judgment call. WIN-041 and WIN-042 in the same group got 'Misleading' for similar re-slicing patterns. WIN-063 could go either way — the re-slicing argument favors 'Misleading,' the mechanism argument favors 'Std Model Explains.' The current verdict is defensible because the mechanism rebuttal (reversed-flux patches, MHD core dynamics) is the more scientifically compelling response. But the text should at minimum note the re-slicing issue.

Overall assessment: This is a well-written entry that successfully addresses the WIN. The Cycle 1 recommendations were largely implemented. The remaining issues are moderate — citation verification and adding the re-slicing argument — not structural problems.

citations_verified: Finlay et al. (2020) DOI 10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9 — DOI resolves (redirects through Springer to Earth, Planets and Space). This is the correct CHAOS-7 paper.

NOAA WMM (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model) — resolves correctly, shows WMM2025 as current version. Page does not specifically discuss hemispheric asymmetry but is a valid general reference for geomagnetic field data.
citations_unchecked: Aubert et al. (2013) doi:10.1038/nature12574 — DOI resolves but target page (nature.com) returned 303 redirect error, preventing content verification. The paper may be 'Bottom-up control of geomagnetic secular variation by the Earth's inner core' rather than a hemispheric-asymmetry-specific study. NEEDS VERIFICATION: does this paper actually discuss hemispheric asymmetry in decay as our text claims, or is it about a different aspect of secular variation? If the latter, a more appropriate citation would be Olson & Deguen (2012, Nature Geoscience, doi:10.1038/ngeo1506) on dipole eccentricity from lopsided inner core growth, or Terra-Nova et al. (2017, PEPI) on reversed-flux patches.
